subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Mirror Formula where, f=focal length, u=distance of the object from mirror, v=distance of image from mirror. Focal length of mirror f = {tex}\frac {R}{2}{/tex} where, R= radius of curvature of mirror {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
text_html:
Mirror Formula
where, f=focal length, u=distance of the object from mirror, v=distance of image from mirror. Focal length of mirror f = {tex}\frac {R}{2}{/tex} where, R= radius of curvature of mirror {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Law of Reflection The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. This law applies to both plane and curved surfaces. Reflection involves the incident ray, reflected ray, and the normal all lying in a common plane.
text_html:
Law of Reflection
The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. This law applies to both plane and curved surfaces. Reflection involves the incident ray, reflected ray, and the normal all lying in a common plane.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Spherical Surfaces Discussion is focused on spherical surfaces. The normal at any point of incidence on these surfaces is along the radius joining the center of curvature to the point.
text_html:
Spherical Surfaces
Discussion is focused on spherical surfaces. The normal at any point of incidence on these surfaces is along the radius joining the center of curvature to the point.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Spherical Mirror Terms The geometric center is called the pole . The center of curvature is denoted by C . The line joining the pole and center of curvature is called the principal axis .
text_html:
Spherical Mirror Terms

  • The geometric center is called the pole.
  • The center of curvature is denoted by C.
  • The line joining the pole and center of curvature is called the principal axis.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Sign Convention Distances measured in the direction of incident light are positive. Heights above the principal axis are positive while those below are negative.
text_html:
Sign Convention
  • Distances measured in the direction of incident light are positive.
  • Heights above the principal axis are positive while those below are negative.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Focal Length of Spherical Mirrors The focal length (f) is half the radius of curvature (R). Hence, f = R/2. The principal focus is where reflected rays converge or appear to diverge.
text_html:
Focal Length of Spherical Mirrors
  • The focal length (f) is half the radius of curvature (R). Hence, f = R/2.
The principal focus is where reflected rays converge or appear to diverge.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Principal Focus and Focal Plane In concave mirrors, the reflected rays converge at the principal focus on the principal axis. The focal plane is perpendicular to the principal axis through the focus.
text_html:
Principal Focus and Focal Plane
  • In concave mirrors, the reflected rays converge at the principal focus on the principal axis.
  • The focal plane is perpendicular to the principal axis through the focus.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Image Formation by Reflection Reflected rays meeting at a point create a real image. When rays appear to diverge from a point, it forms a virtual image.
text_html:
Image Formation by Reflection
  • Reflected rays meeting at a point create a real image. When rays appear to diverge from a point, it forms a virtual image.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Ray Diagram for Concave Mirror Rays parallel to principal axis go through the focus after reflection. Rays through the center of curvature retrace the path after reflection.
text_html:
Ray Diagram for Concave Mirror
  • Rays parallel to principal axis go through the focus after reflection.
  • Rays through the center of curvature retrace the path after reflection.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Mirror Equation Derived as {tex}\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex} , relating object distance (u), image distance (v), and focal length (f). Applicable to both real and virtual images.
text_html:
Mirror Equation
Derived as {tex}\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex}, relating object distance (u), image distance (v), and focal length (f). Applicable to both real and virtual images.

subject: Physics
topic: Reflection of Light at Spherical and Plane Mirror
text: Magnification Formula Expressed as {tex}m = \frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u}{/tex} , where h' is the image height and h is the object height. Applies universally for any image type (real or virtual).
text_html:
Magnification Formula
Expressed as {tex}m = \frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u}{/tex}, where h' is the image height and h is the object height. Applies universally for any image type (real or virtual).

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Laws of Refraction
text_html: Laws of Refraction

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: incident ray, refracted ray and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant for the two given media. This constant is denoted by n and is called the relative refractive index. n = {tex}\frac {sin \ i}{sin \ r}{/tex} (Snell’s law) where, n is refractive index of the second medium when first medium is vacuum or air. Refractive index of medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1 is denoted as n 21 and defined as n 21 = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}= \frac {\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} where, v 1 , {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} are speed and wavelength of light in the first medium, similarly v 2 and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} for second medium. n 1 and n 2 are absolute refractive indices of medium1 and medium 2 respectively. Mathematically, absolute refractive index is given by n = {tex}\frac{\text { speed of light in vacuum }}{\text { speed of light in the material }}=\frac{c}{v}{/tex}
text_html:
1. incident ray, refracted ray and normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
  2. The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant for the two given media. This constant is denoted by n and is called the relative refractive index.

n = {tex}\frac {sin \ i}{sin \ r}{/tex}(Snell’s law)
where, n is refractive index of the second medium when first medium is vacuum or air. Refractive index of medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1 is denoted as n21 and defined as n21 = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}= \frac {\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} where, v1, {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} are speed and wavelength of light in the first medium, similarly v2 and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} for second medium. n1 and n2 are absolute refractive indices of medium1 and medium 2 respectively.
Mathematically, absolute refractive index is given by n = {tex}\frac{\text { speed of light in vacuum }}{\text { speed of light in the material }}=\frac{c}{v}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Reflection and Refraction A beam of light can reflect back into the first medium or enter another medium, changing direction at the interface, known as refraction.
text_html:
Reflection and Refraction
A beam of light can reflect back into the first medium or enter another medium, changing direction at the interface, known as refraction.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Incident and Refracted Rays A ray of light represents a beam, and when entering another medium, the angle of incidence with the normal changes to the angle of refraction.
text_html:
Incident and Refracted Rays
A ray of light represents a beam, and when entering another medium, the angle of incidence with the normal changes to the angle of refraction.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Snell's Law The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of angle of refraction is constant: {tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=n_{21} {/tex}
text_html:
Snell's Law
The ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of angle of refraction is constant: {tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=n_{21} {/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Refractive Index The refractive index, {tex} n_{21} {/tex} , is characteristic of the two media pair and affects how light bends.
text_html:
Refractive Index
The refractive index, {tex} n_{21} {/tex}, is characteristic of the two media pair and affects how light bends.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Optically Denser Medium If {tex}{/tex} n_{21}>1, r Optically denser than medium 1.
text_html:
Optically Denser Medium

  • If {tex}{/tex} n_{21}>1, r
  • Optically denser than medium 1.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Rarer Medium If {tex} n_{21}<1, r>i {/tex} : Refracted ray bends away from the normal. Incident ray goes from denser to rarer medium.
text_html:
Rarer Medium

  • If {tex} n_{21}<1, r>i {/tex}: Refracted ray bends away from the normal.
  • Incident ray goes from denser to rarer medium.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Optical Density vs. Mass Density Optical density does not correlate directly with mass density; example: turpentine and water.
text_html:
Optical Density vs. Mass Density
Optical density does not correlate directly with mass density; example: turpentine and water.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Snell's Law Application If {tex} n_{12} = \frac{1}{n_{21} } {/tex} : The refractive index is reciprocal between two media. {tex} n_{32}= n_{31} \times n_{12} {/tex} : For three media interface.
text_html:
Snell's Law Application

  • If {tex} n_{12} = \frac{1}{n_{21} } {/tex}: The refractive index is reciprocal between two media.
  • {tex} n_{32}= n_{31} \times n_{12} {/tex}: For three media interface.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Lateral Shift Refracted rays through parallel-sided slabs experience lateral displacement but remain parallel to the incident ray.
text_html:
Lateral Shift
Refracted rays through parallel-sided slabs experience lateral displacement but remain parallel to the incident ray.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction
text: Apparent Depth For perpendicular viewing: Apparent depth is the real depth divided by the medium's refractive index.
text_html:
Apparent Depth
For perpendicular viewing: Apparent depth is the real depth divided by the medium's refractive index.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Light Travel through Media When light moves from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium, part of it reflects back into the original medium, while the other part refracts into the second medium. This is known as internal reflection. Example ray: {tex} {AO}_1 {~B} {/tex}
text_html:
Light Travel through Media
When light moves from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium, part of it reflects back into the original medium, while the other part refracts into the second medium. This is known as internal reflection.

  • Example ray: {tex} {AO}_1 {~B} {/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Refraction and Internal Reflection As light enters a rarer medium from a denser medium, it bends away from the normal. The angle of refraction (r) is larger than the angle of incidence (i). When the angle of incidence increases enough, the ray grazes the surface between the two media.
text_html:
Refraction and Internal Reflection
As light enters a rarer medium from a denser medium, it bends away from the normal. The angle of refraction (r) is larger than the angle of incidence (i). When the angle of incidence increases enough, the ray grazes the surface between the two media.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Total Internal Reflection When the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle, refraction doesn't occur, leading to total internal reflection. During total internal reflection, there is no light transmission, and the ray is fully reflected back.
text_html:
Total Internal Reflection
When the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle, refraction doesn't occur, leading to total internal reflection. During total internal reflection, there is no light transmission, and the ray is fully reflected back.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Critical Angle and Snell's Law The critical angle for a medium pair is when the refracted angle is {tex} 90^{\circ} {/tex} , defined as {tex} i_c {/tex} . Snell's Law states: {tex} \sin i_c = n_{21} {/tex} An upper limit exists for which Snell's Law is applicable; no refraction occurs beyond it.
text_html:
Critical Angle and Snell's Law
The critical angle for a medium pair is when the refracted angle is {tex} 90^{\circ} {/tex}, defined as {tex} i_c {/tex}. Snell's Law states:

  • {tex} \sin i_c = n_{21} {/tex}

An upper limit exists for which Snell's Law is applicable; no refraction occurs beyond it.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Refractive Index and Critical Angle The refractive index of a denser medium in relation to a rarer medium is given by: {tex} n_{12}=1 / \sin i_{{c}} {/tex} Common critical angles include: Water: 48.75 Crown glass: 41.14 Diamond: 24.41
text_html:
Refractive Index and Critical Angle
The refractive index of a denser medium in relation to a rarer medium is given by:

  • {tex} n_{12}=1 / \sin i_{{c}} {/tex}

Common critical angles include:

  • Water: 48.75
  • Crown glass: 41.14
  • Diamond: 24.41

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Demonstration of Total Internal Reflection Using a laser torch, a turbidity-inducing substance like milk in water can demonstrate total internal reflection. Adjust the beam until no refraction occurs, and the beam is wholly reflected.
text_html:
Demonstration of Total Internal Reflection
Using a laser torch, a turbidity-inducing substance like milk in water can demonstrate total internal reflection. Adjust the beam until no refraction occurs, and the beam is wholly reflected.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Applications of Total Internal Reflection: Prisms Prisms that bend light by 90° or 180° employ total internal reflection. These can also invert images without size changes. These applications depend on the critical angle being less than 45°.
text_html:
Applications of Total Internal Reflection: Prisms
Prisms that bend light by 90° or 180° employ total internal reflection. These can also invert images without size changes.
  • These applications depend on the critical angle being less than 45°.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Optical Fibres Optical fibres use total internal reflection for long-distance transmission of signals. Here: A core and cladding design maximize total internal reflection. The core has a higher refractive index than the cladding, optimizing signal transmission.
text_html:
Optical Fibres
Optical fibres use total internal reflection for long-distance transmission of signals. Here:
  • A core and cladding design maximize total internal reflection.
  • The core has a higher refractive index than the cladding, optimizing signal transmission.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Purity and Preparation of Optical Fibres To minimize light absorption, optical fibres require purified quartz materials, transmitting over 95% light per kilometer, unlike ordinary glass.
text_html:
Purity and Preparation of Optical Fibres
To minimize light absorption, optical fibres require purified quartz materials, transmitting over 95% light per kilometer, unlike ordinary glass.

subject: Physics
topic: Total Internal Reflection and its Applications
text: Decorative and Medical Uses of Optical Fibres Used for visual inspections and lighting decoratively: Light pipes help examine internal organs like the esophagus. Fountain-like fibre lamps show light at the free ends of plastic fibres.
text_html:
Decorative and Medical Uses of Optical Fibres
Used for visual inspections and lighting decoratively:
  • Light pipes help examine internal organs like the esophagus.
  • Fountain-like fibre lamps show light at the free ends of plastic fibres.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Linear Magnification: The ratio of the size of the image formed by a spherical mirror I to the size of the object O is called the linear magnification produced by the spherical mirror. {tex}m=\frac{I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{f}{f-u}=\frac{f-v}{f}{/tex} Where, I=height of image and O= height of object, it is negative corresponding to real image and positive for virtual image.
text_html:
Linear Magnification: The ratio of the size of the image formed by a spherical mirror I to the size of the object O is called the linear magnification produced by the spherical mirror.
{tex}m=\frac{I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{f}{f-u}=\frac{f-v}{f}{/tex}
Where, I=height of image and O= height of object, it is negative corresponding to real image and positive for virtual image.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Principle of reversibility of light states that when final path of a ray of light after any number of reflections and refractions is reversed, the ray retraces its entire path. n 21 {tex}\times{/tex} n 12 = 1 or n 21 = {tex}\frac {1}{n_{12}}{/tex} & Refractive index, n = {tex}\frac{\text { real depth }}{\text { apparent depth }}{/tex}
text_html:
Principle of reversibility of light states that when final path of a ray of light after any number of reflections and refractions is reversed, the ray retraces its entire path.
n21 {tex}\times{/tex} n12 = 1 or n21 = {tex}\frac {1}{n_{12}}{/tex}
& Refractive index, n = {tex}\frac{\text { real depth }}{\text { apparent depth }}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Total Internal Reflection: (TIR) When a ray of light travelling from denser medium to rarer medium is incident at the interface of two medium at an angle greater than the critical angle for the two media, the ray is totally reflected back to denser medium. This phenomenon is called Total Internal Reflection. It occurs only when angle of incidence in denser medium is greater (not equal) than critical angle, i.e. i > ic Optical fibre, mirage, sparkling of diamond, totally reflecting prism, etc. work on the principle of total internal reflection.
text_html: Total Internal Reflection: (TIR) When a ray of light travelling from denser medium to rarer medium is incident at the interface of two medium at an angle greater than the critical angle for the two media, the ray is totally reflected back to denser medium. This phenomenon is called Total Internal Reflection. It occurs only when angle of incidence in denser medium is greater (not equal) than critical angle, i.e. i > ic Optical fibre, mirage, sparkling of diamond, totally reflecting prism, etc. work on the principle of total internal reflection.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Critical Angle (i c )- It is the angle of incidence in denser medium for which angle of refraction in rarer medium is 900. i.e. n = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin i_c}{/tex}
text_html: Critical Angle (ic)- It is the angle of incidence in denser medium for which angle of refraction in rarer medium is 900. i.e. n = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin i_c}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Refraction formula for refraction by convex or concave spherical refracting surface is given by {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} where n 1 , n 2 are reflective indices of rarer and denser media respectively and u, v & R are to be taken with proper signs. When refraction takes place denser to rarer medium, then {tex}\frac{n_1}{v}-\frac{n_2}{u}=\frac{n_1-n_2}{R}{/tex}
text_html:
Refraction formula for refraction by convex or concave spherical refracting surface is given by {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex} where n1, n2 are reflective indices of rarer and denser media respectively and u, v & R are to be taken with proper signs.
When refraction takes place denser to rarer medium, then {tex}\frac{n_1}{v}-\frac{n_2}{u}=\frac{n_1-n_2}{R}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Lens maker’s formula: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(n_{21}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}
text_html: Lens maker’s formula: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\left(n_{21}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Power of Lens: The ability of a lens to converge or diverge the rays of light incident on it is called the power of the lens. P = {tex}\frac{1}{f(\text { in } m)}{/tex} S.I. unit of power of lens is dioptre (D) = m -1 Power of combination lenses in contact is given by P = P 1 + P 2 +...+P n OR, {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}+\cdots \ldots+\frac{1}{f_n}{/tex}
text_html:
Power of Lens: The ability of a lens to converge or diverge the rays of light incident on it is called the power of the lens.
P = {tex}\frac{1}{f(\text { in } m)}{/tex} S.I. unit of power of lens is dioptre (D) = m-1
Power of combination lenses in contact is given by
P = P1 + P2 +...+Pn
OR,
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}+\cdots \ldots+\frac{1}{f_n}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Refraction at Spherical Interface - Refraction occurs at the interface where two transparent media meet. - A spherical surface can be treated like a plane for small segments. - Normal at the point of incidence is perpendicular to the tangent at that point.
text_html: Refraction at Spherical Interface - Refraction occurs at the interface where two transparent media meet. - A spherical surface can be treated like a plane for small segments. - Normal at the point of incidence is perpendicular to the tangent at that point.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Image Formation at Spherical Surface - Formation involves object, image, and refractive indexes of media. - Refraction follows Snell's Law: n1 sin i = n2 sin r. - Equation for image distance: {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}
text_html: Image Formation at Spherical Surface - Formation involves object, image, and refractive indexes of media. - Refraction follows Snell's Law: n1 sin i = n2 sin r. - Equation for image distance: {tex}\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Thin Lenses - Lenses have two surfaces, one or both surfaces should be spherical. - Lens formula is derived using image formation from spherical surfaces. - Fundamental lens equations applicable for real and virtual images.
text_html: Thin Lenses - Lenses have two surfaces, one or both surfaces should be spherical. - Lens formula is derived using image formation from spherical surfaces. - Fundamental lens equations applicable for real and virtual images.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Spherical Lens Geometry - Double convex lens forms images by refracting light at two surfaces. - Image from the first surface acts as a virtual object for the second. - Composite image equations derived by combining first and second refractions.
text_html: Spherical Lens Geometry - Double convex lens forms images by refracting light at two surfaces. - Image from the first surface acts as a virtual object for the second. - Composite image equations derived by combining first and second refractions.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Lens Maker's Formula - It predicts focal length from radii of curvature and refractive indices. - Refers to thin lenses: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n_{21}-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} - Concave lenses have negative focal lengths due to radius conventions.
text_html: Lens Maker's Formula - It predicts focal length from radii of curvature and refractive indices. - Refers to thin lenses: {tex}\frac{1}{f}=(n_{21}-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right){/tex} - Concave lenses have negative focal lengths due to radius conventions.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Refraction Ray Tracing - Optical phenomena through lenses uses several ray tracing principles. Ray parallel to the axis refracts through focal point. Ray through optical center remains undeviated. Ray through focus becomes parallel post-refraction.
text_html:
Refraction Ray Tracing - Optical phenomena through lenses uses several ray tracing principles.
  • Ray parallel to the axis refracts through focal point.
  • Ray through optical center remains undeviated.
  • Ray through focus becomes parallel post-refraction.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Ray Diagrams - Useful for visualizing image formation through lenses. - Requires practice drawing different object positions and verifying lens formulas. - Demonstrates convergence or divergence by lens type.
text_html: Ray Diagrams - Useful for visualizing image formation through lenses. - Requires practice drawing different object positions and verifying lens formulas. - Demonstrates convergence or divergence by lens type.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Magnification by Lenses - Magnification (m) is the ratio of image height to object height. - Equations relate distances: {tex}m = \frac{h'}{h}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} - Positive magnification implies erect images; negative indicates inverted images.
text_html: Magnification by Lenses - Magnification (m) is the ratio of image height to object height. - Equations relate distances: {tex}m = \frac{h'}{h}=\frac{v}{u}{/tex} - Positive magnification implies erect images; negative indicates inverted images.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Power of a Lens - Defines the lens's ability to converge or diverge light. - Measured in diopters (D), proportional to inverse focal length. - Useful property in corrective eyewear design.
text_html: Power of a Lens - Defines the lens's ability to converge or diverge light. - Measured in diopters (D), proportional to inverse focal length. - Useful property in corrective eyewear design.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces and by Lenses
text: Focal Points of Lenses - Lenses have two principal foci: on either side. - First focal point is closer to light source; second is further away. - Equidistant from optical center in double convex/concave lenses.
text_html: Focal Points of Lenses - Lenses have two principal foci: on either side. - First focal point is closer to light source; second is further away. - Equidistant from optical center in double convex/concave lenses.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Magnification by combination of lenses is given by m = m 1 {tex}\times{/tex} m 2 {tex}\times{/tex} m 3 ... Prism have got the property of bending the incident light towards its base. A prism is a portion of a transparent medium bounded by two plane faces inclined to each other at suitable angle. When the prism is adjusted at angle of minimum deviation, then angle of incidence is equal to the angle of emergence i 1 = i 2 , r 1 = r 2 , {tex}\delta=\boldsymbol{\delta}_{\mathbf{m}}{/tex} = 2i and 2r = A n = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex} = (n - 1)A for small angle of prism
text_html:
Magnification by combination of lenses is given by m = m1 {tex}\times{/tex} m2 {tex}\times{/tex} m3...

  1. Prism have got the property of bending the incident light towards its base. A prism is a portion of a transparent medium bounded by two plane faces inclined to each other at suitable angle.
  2. When the prism is adjusted at angle of minimum deviation, then
    1. angle of incidence is equal to the angle of emergence
    2. i1 = i2, r1 = r2, {tex}\delta=\boldsymbol{\delta}_{\mathbf{m}}{/tex} = 2i and 2r = A
    3. n = {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\delta \mathrm{m}{/tex} = (n - 1)A for small angle of prism

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Dispersion by a Prism The phenomenon of splitting of light into its component colours is known dispersion. Angular Dispersion Angular dispersion produced by a prism for white l the angles of deviation for two extreme colours i.e. violet and red. Angular deviations for violet {tex}\delta_v{/tex} = (n v - 1)A For Red, {tex}\delta_R{/tex} = (n R - 1)A and for Yellow {tex}\delta_Y{/tex} = (n Y - 1)A Dispersive power, {tex}\omega=\frac{n_v-n_R}{n_y-1}=\frac{\delta_v-\delta_R}{\delta_y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {Angular \ dispersion}{ Mean \ deviation}{/tex} Where, {tex}\delta_y{/tex} = mean deviation or deviation for yellow colour Angular dispersion = {tex}\delta_v - ​​\delta_R{/tex}
text_html:
Dispersion by a Prism The phenomenon of splitting of light into its component colours is known dispersion.
Angular Dispersion Angular dispersion produced by a prism for white l the angles of deviation for two extreme colours i.e. violet and red.
Angular deviations for violet {tex}\delta_v{/tex} = (nv - 1)A
For Red, {tex}\delta_R{/tex} = (nR - 1)A and for Yellow {tex}\delta_Y{/tex} = (nY - 1)A
Dispersive power, {tex}\omega=\frac{n_v-n_R}{n_y-1}=\frac{\delta_v-\delta_R}{\delta_y}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {Angular \ dispersion}{ Mean \ deviation}{/tex}
Where, {tex}\delta_y{/tex} = mean deviation or deviation for yellow colour
Angular dispersion = {tex}\delta_v - ​​\delta_R{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Concept of Refraction through a Prism A ray of light changes direction when passing through a triangular glass prism, due to refraction at the prism faces AB and AC.
text_html: Concept of Refraction through a Prism A ray of light changes direction when passing through a triangular glass prism, due to refraction at the prism faces AB and AC.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Incident and Refracted Angles - The angle of incidence at the first face AB is i. - The angle of refraction is r 1 . - The angle of incidence at the second face AC is r 2 . - At emergence, the angle of refraction is e.
text_html: Incident and Refracted Angles - The angle of incidence at the first face AB is i. - The angle of refraction is r1. - The angle of incidence at the second face AC is r2. - At emergence, the angle of refraction is e.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Angle of Deviation - The emergent ray RS forms an angle δ with the incident ray PQ. - It is known as the angle of deviation.
text_html: Angle of Deviation - The emergent ray RS forms an angle δ with the incident ray PQ. - It is known as the angle of deviation.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Quadrilateral AQNR Angle Sum - In quadrilateral AQNR, angles at vertices Q and R are right angles. - Hence, the sum of other angles is 180°: - A + QNR = 180°.
text_html: Quadrilateral AQNR Angle Sum - In quadrilateral AQNR, angles at vertices Q and R are right angles. - Hence, the sum of other angles is 180°:  - A + QNR = 180°.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Triangle QNR Angle Sum - In triangle QNR: - r 1 + r 2 + QNR = 180°. - Comparing two equations gives: - r 1 + r 2 = A.
text_html: Triangle QNR Angle Sum - In triangle QNR:  - r1 + r2 + QNR = 180°. - Comparing two equations gives:  - r1 + r2 = A.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Total Deviation Formula δ = (i - r 1 ) + (e - r 2 ) It simplifies to: δ = i + e - A.
text_html: Total Deviation Formula δ = (i - r1) + (e - r2) It simplifies to: δ = i + e - A.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Symmetry in Deviation For any given δ (except when i = e), there are two values of incidence angle i (hence e), due to symmetry in equation δ = i + e - A.
text_html: Symmetry in Deviation For any given δ (except when i = e), there are two values of incidence angle i (hence e), due to symmetry in equation δ = i + e - A.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Minimum Deviation and Parallel Refracted Ray - At minimum deviation D m , the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to prism's base. - i = e implies r 1 = r 2 .
text_html: Minimum Deviation and Parallel Refracted Ray - At minimum deviation Dm, the refracted ray inside the prism becomes parallel to prism's base. - i = e implies r1 = r2.

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Refractive Index Equation The refractive index n 21 is calculated as: - n 21 = sin[(A + D m ) / 2] / sin[A / 2]. For thin prisms, it is approximated to: - n 21 ≈ (A + D m ) / 2 / (A / 2).
text_html: Refractive Index Equation The refractive index n21 is calculated as:  - n21 = sin[(A + Dm) / 2] / sin[A / 2]. For thin prisms, it is approximated to:  - n21 ≈ (A + Dm) / 2 / (A / 2).

subject: Physics
topic: Refraction of Light Through a Prism
text: Implication for Thin Prisms For thin prisms, D m is small: - D m = (n 21 - 1) A, implying little deviation of light.
text_html: Implication for Thin Prisms For thin prisms, Dm is small:  - Dm = (n21 - 1) A, implying little deviation of light.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Magnifying power of simple microscope- when final image is formed at D, m = 1 + {tex}\frac {D}{ f}{/tex} when final image is formed at infinity m = {tex}\frac {D}{f}{/tex} Magnifying power of compound microscope- when final image at D, m = {tex}\frac{v_0}{-u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex} when final image at infinity m = {tex}\frac{L D}{f_o f_e}{/tex} Magnifying power of Telescope- when final image is formed at D, m = {tex}\frac{-f_0}{f_e}\left(1+\frac{f_e}{D}\right){/tex} Length of tube L = f o + {tex}\frac{f_e D}{f_e+D}{/tex} when final image at infinity m = {tex}\frac{-f_o}{f_e}{/tex} Length of tube L = f o + f e
text_html:
Magnifying power of simple microscope-

  1. when final image is formed at D, m = 1 + {tex}\frac {D}{ f}{/tex}
  2. when final image is formed at infinity m = {tex}\frac {D}{f}{/tex}

Magnifying power of compound microscope-

  1. when final image at D, m = {tex}\frac{v_0}{-u_0}\left(1+\frac{D}{f_e}\right){/tex}
  2. when final image at infinity m = {tex}\frac{L D}{f_o f_e}{/tex}

Magnifying power of Telescope-

  1. when final image is formed at D, m = {tex}\frac{-f_0}{f_e}\left(1+\frac{f_e}{D}\right){/tex}
    Length of tube L = fo + {tex}\frac{f_e D}{f_e+D}{/tex}
  2. when final image at infinity m = {tex}\frac{-f_o}{f_e}{/tex}
    Length of tube L = fo + fe

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Advantage of reflecting type telescope- Reflecting type telescopes using parabolic mirrors are free from chromatic and spherical aberrations. Hence sharp image of object is formed. The parabolic mirrors of large aperture can be easily manufactured. Reflecting type telescopes has good light gathering power and good resolving power.
text_html:
Advantage of reflecting type telescope-

  1. Reflecting type telescopes using parabolic mirrors are free from chromatic and spherical aberrations. Hence sharp image of object is formed.
  2. The parabolic mirrors of large aperture can be easily manufactured.
  3. Reflecting type telescopes has good light gathering power and good resolving power.

text: Important ray diagrams- Simple Microscope in relax vision Simple Microscope in distinct vision

  2. Simple Microscope in distinct vision

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Optical Devices and Their Uses Devices such as periscopes, kaleidoscopes, binoculars, telescopes, and microscopes use reflecting and refracting properties of lenses, mirrors, and prisms.
text_html:
Optical Devices and Their Uses
Devices such as periscopes, kaleidoscopes, binoculars, telescopes, and microscopes use reflecting and refracting properties of lenses, mirrors, and prisms.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: The Human Eye The eye is an optical device designed by nature, enabling us to perceive the world around us.
text_html:
The Human Eye
The eye is an optical device designed by nature, enabling us to perceive the world around us.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Principles of Microscopes A simple microscope uses a converging lens of small focal length to magnify objects when the object is within the lens' focal distance.
text_html:
Principles of Microscopes
A simple microscope uses a converging lens of small focal length to magnify objects when the object is within the lens' focal distance.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Simple Microscope Magnification The magnification formula for a simple microscope at the near point is m = (1 + D/f) , where D is the near point distance, and f is the lens' focal length.
text_html:
Simple Microscope Magnification
The magnification formula for a simple microscope at the near point is m = (1 + D/f), where D is the near point distance, and f is the lens' focal length.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Compound Microscope In a compound microscope, the objective lens creates a magnified, inverted image that is further magnified by the eyepiece, resulting in higher magnification.
text_html:
Compound Microscope
In a compound microscope, the objective lens creates a magnified, inverted image that is further magnified by the eyepiece, resulting in higher magnification.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Compound Microscope Magnification Formula: m = (L/f_o) \times (D/f_e) , where L is the distance between the lenses, f_o is the objective's focal length, and f_e is the eyepiece's focal length.
text_html:
Compound Microscope Magnification
Formula: m = (L/f_o) \times (D/f_e), where L is the distance between the lenses, f_o is the objective's focal length, and f_e is the eyepiece's focal length.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Telescope Purpose and Function Telescopes magnify distant objects by concentrating light through an objective lens, creating a final inverted image.
text_html:
Telescope Purpose and Function
Telescopes magnify distant objects by concentrating light through an objective lens, creating a final inverted image.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Telescope Magnifying Power Magnification, m , of a telescope is determined by m = f_o/f_e , where f_o and f_e are the focal lengths of the objective and eyepiece, respectively.
text_html:
Telescope Magnifying Power
Magnification, m, of a telescope is determined by m = f_o/f_e, where f_o and f_e are the focal lengths of the objective and eyepiece, respectively.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Reflecting Telescopes Modern telescopes use concave mirrors to avoid chromatic aberration and allow structural support, enhancing quality and ease of maintenance.
text_html:
Reflecting Telescopes
Modern telescopes use concave mirrors to avoid chromatic aberration and allow structural support, enhancing quality and ease of maintenance.

subject: Physics
topic: Optical Instruments
text: Cassegrain Telescope This design incorporates a secondary convex mirror to reflect light through a hole in the primary mirror, offering compactness and increased focal length in shorter tubes.
text_html:
Cassegrain Telescope
This design incorporates a secondary convex mirror to reflect light through a hole in the primary mirror, offering compactness and increased focal length in shorter tubes.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Sensitivity of the Human Eye The retina of the human eye is sensitive to detect electromagnetic waves within a limited range of the electromagnetic spectrum, specifically from 400 nm to 750 nm, known as light.
text_html:
Sensitivity of the Human Eye
The retina of the human eye is sensitive to detect electromagnetic waves within a limited range of the electromagnetic spectrum, specifically from 400 nm to 750 nm, known as light.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Light and Vision Light allows humans to interpret the world through vision, thanks to its electromagnetic properties.
text_html:
Light and Vision
Light allows humans to interpret the world through vision, thanks to its electromagnetic properties.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Speed of Light Licht travels with immense speed. The speed of light in a vacuum is accepted as c = 2.99792458 × 10 8 m/s , typically approximated to 3 × 10 8 m/s .
text_html:
Speed of Light
Licht travels with immense speed. The speed of light in a vacuum is accepted as c = 2.99792458 × 108 m/s, typically approximated to 3 × 108 m/s.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Nature of Light Travel Light travels in a straight line, despite being an electromagnetic wave. This straight-line path is called a ray.
text_html:
Nature of Light Travel
Light travels in a straight line, despite being an electromagnetic wave. This straight-line path is called a ray.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Ray of Light - A ray of light is the path that light follows. - A collection of rays is known as a beam of light.
text_html:
Ray of Light
- A ray of light is the path that light follows.
- A collection of rays is known as a beam of light.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Reflection, Refraction, and Dispersion This chapter studies these three phenomena using the ray model of light.
text_html:
Reflection, Refraction, and Dispersion
This chapter studies these three phenomena using the ray model of light.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Laws of Reflection and Refraction Examine the laws governing reflection and refraction to understand image formation by various surfaces.
text_html:
Laws of Reflection and Refraction
Examine the laws governing reflection and refraction to understand image formation by various surfaces.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Image Formation Study image formation by both: Plane surfaces Spherical reflecting and refracting surfaces
text_html:
Image Formation
Study image formation by both:
  • Plane surfaces
  • Spherical reflecting and refracting surfaces

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Optical Instruments The chapter also delves into the construction and working of key optical instruments, including insights into the human eye.
text_html:
Optical Instruments
The chapter also delves into the construction and working of key optical instruments, including insights into the human eye.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Reconciliation of Light Concepts The small wavelength of light compared to everyday objects explains the seeming contradiction between light's wave nature and straight-line travel.
text_html:
Reconciliation of Light Concepts
The small wavelength of light compared to everyday objects explains the seeming contradiction between light's wave nature and straight-line travel.
